result:
The old woman at the farmers market sells honey in mason jars with handwritten labels, and every Saturday she tells me the same story about her bees. How they know her footsteps. How they dance their directions to the wildflower fields beyond the highway. Today she adds something new: "They're dreaming of winter already. I can tell by how they fly—a little slower, a little heavier, like they're carrying next season's sleep in their wings."

I buy three jars because her hands shake now when she makes change, and I want her to go home early, rest those swollen knuckles. But really, I buy them because I need to believe in something as simple as bees knowing footsteps.

The subway home is delayed. Signal problems, they announce, which means nothing and everything. A mariachi band works through the stopped cars, accordion breathing between stations, and a child drops her ice cream cone, watches it roll across the dirty floor with such profound betrayal in her eyes that her mother buys her another one from the platform vendor even though it's almost dinner time. Small rebellions against Tuesday afternoon tragedy.

I've been thinking about time lately—how it moves like honey when you're young, then water, then wind. My nephew asked me last week what it feels like to be old. I'm only thirty-six, but to him, I might as well be ancient. I told him it feels like being young but with more ghosts. He didn't understand. How could he? His ghosts haven't been born yet.

There's a particular quality of light that only exists at 4:17 PM in October, when the sun hits the kitchen window and turns everything gold and dusty, like the inside of an antique shop. My grandmother's china catches it, the plates I never use but can't give away, and for a moment I can smell her cigarettes and lavender perfume, hear her humming off-key while washing dishes. She's been gone twelve years. The plates haven't moved.

Someone on the internet wrote that we die twice—once when we stop breathing, and again when someone says our name for the last time. But I think we die a little bit every day, shedding old selves like snake skin, leaving behind versions of ourselves in places we'll never return to. The girl who cried over failed algebra. The woman who said yes to the wrong ring. The person who stood at the intersection of Broadway and 42nd, overwhelmed by the noise and possibility, wondering if this was what happiness looked like or just its loud cousin.

The honey woman's bees are already dreaming of winter. What do bees dream? Hexagons and heat, maybe. The democracy of the swarm. Their queen's pheromones like a song only they can hear. Or maybe they dream of being anything but bees—eagles or whales or humans who buy honey in mason jars and pretend to understand stories about insects carrying seasons in their wings.

My neighbor is learning violin. She's terrible, but she practices every evening at seven, and there's something hopeful about those screeling notes floating through the walls. She's seventy-three, started last month. "Why now?" I asked her in the hallway. "Why not?" she said, and I couldn't think of a single good answer.

This is what I know: The world is ending and beginning every second. Children are being born while others take their last breath. Someone is falling in love in a coffee shop while someone else signs divorce papers. The Arctic is melting. Somewhere, a seed that's been dormant for decades suddenly decides to grow. These things are equally true and equally important, and if you think about it too much, you'll never get out of bed.

But you do get out of bed. You buy honey. You listen to bad violin. You save voice messages from people you love, just in case. You water the plants even though you suspect they're already dead. You write thank-you notes. You learn the names of your barista's cats. You hold the door. You overtip. You text "thinking of you" and mean it. You make soup from scratch. You call your mother.

The light is fading now, that golden 4:17 moment passing into regular evening. Rush hour is starting. People are hurrying home to their own kitchens, their own ghosts, their own china they never use. Tomorrow the honey woman will be at the market again if it doesn't rain, telling someone else about her bees, how they know her footsteps, how they're already dreaming of winter.

Maybe that's enough—to be known by bees, to carry seasons in our wings, to practice violin badly at seventy-three. To buy three jars of honey from an old woman with swollen knuckles and believe, really believe, that somewhere in those amber jars is the distilled essence of ten thousand flowers, the work of creatures who dream in hexagons, the promise that even when winter comes, there will be sweetness stored away, waiting.

The mariachi music echoes in the subway tunnel. The child with her new ice cream smiles. We move forward through the signal problems, toward our various homes, carrying our Tuesdays like careful cargo, like bees heavy with tomorrow's sleep.
